question: Explain the order of constructor execution when a single class is instantiated. What is the very first step the JVM takes when the 'new' keyword is encountered?
expectedPoints: Memory is allocated for the new object on the heap.
Default initialization of instance variables occurs (primitives to 0/false, references to null).
The specific constructor's code block is executed, which may include calls to other constructors (this() or super()).

question: Describe the complete chain of constructor execution in a multi-level class hierarchy (e.g., Grandparent -> Parent -> Child). Why does the constructor of the base class (like Object) always execute first?
expectedPoints: Execution begins with the constructor of the bottom-most child class.
The first line of any constructor is an implicit call to super(), which chains up to the immediate parent's constructor.
This chain continues recursively up to the java.lang.Object constructor, which executes first.
After Object's constructor completes, each subsequent constructor in the hierarchy executes downward, finishing with the child's constructor.

question: A programmer writes an explicit call to this() as the first line in a constructor. Analyze the subsequent flow of execution and explain how this affects the initialization process. Compare this to a constructor that begins with an explicit call to super().
expectedPoints: A call to this() invokes another constructor within the same class, changing the typical upward chain to a lateral one first.
The invoked constructor (via this()) will then implicitly or explicitly call super(), beginning the upward chain to the parent.
This means the entire class's constructors are essentially 'initialized' from the one that ultimately calls super(), before any of their own code runs.
In contrast, a call to super() directly begins the upward chain immediately, following the standard hierarchy.
In both cases, the rule that another constructor must be the first statement is enforced, ensuring a single, unambiguous path for initialization.
